abstract:
This proposal is in the area of Computational Social Sciences, and in particular of large-scale social network inference and analysis. It has two overarching goals:

To develop principled mathematical and computational methods for inferring social networks from large heterogeneous datasets (WP1); To apply these methods in large-scale sociological studies, in particular, to the following 3 workpackages: WP2: to identify network-based mechanisms explaining social mobility using data from the Swedish register; WP3: to investigate the role that the social network of immigrants has on job recruitment; and WP4: to explain how networked image-based political narratives spread in social media.

These two goals are strictly interlinked: the development of novel computational methods is motivated and driven by the practical sociological questions, while a thorough sociological analysis in the applicative workpackages is in turn enabled by the algorithms.

The scientists participating in the consortium bring together expertise from both Computational Sciences and Social Sciences, coherently with the interdisciplinary nature of the proposed research.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 22,142,780 SEK
title: Social network inference from complex digital data: methods and applications
title-short: Social network inference from complex digital data

abstract:
Despite decades of immigration, many ethnic groups in Sweden remain socially and spatially separated. Rising diversity has not guaranteed rising integration—particularly between migrant-origin groups. This 3-year project investigates how segregation shapes social boundaries by studying patterns of partner choice.

Romantic unions are powerful indicators of group relations: they reflect whether ethnic boundaries are weakening or holding firm, and are deeply shaped by the segregated contexts in which people live. Yet most research frames ethnic boundaries either as a binary between natives and immigrants, or as a question of in-group versus out-group partnering—rarely examining how immigrant-origin groups relate to each other.

Using Swedish register data from 1991 to 2022, combined with high-resolution geographic information, we model how residential segregation and group composition structure the partner market. Through discrete choice models and counterfactual simulations, we estimate the strength of social boundaries between specific groups, distinguishing structural constraints from active social preferences.

By revealing where boundaries persist even when people live nearby—and where they dissolve despite segregation—this project offers a timely, detailed understanding of how segregation and social distance are linked. As spatial and social divisions continue to shape life chances, understanding intergroup relations is critical for building an inclusive society.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Understanding Social Boundaries through Partner Choice: The Role of Residential Segregation
title-short: Understanding Social Boundaries through Partner Choice

abstract:
People and the contexts they occupy vary in many dimensions---ethnicity, gender, income, education---that shape life experiences, social ties, aspirations, and opportunities. Segregation research examines how the composition of contexts varies across times, places, and individuals. But it often examines contextual variations in a single dimension at a time, e.g., focusing on ethnic segregation separately from income segregation. This overlooks how these aspects of segregation overlap and intersect. Macro-structural social theory and social network research suggest the overlaps and intersections between different social characteristics---referred to as consolidation--are critical to processes of stratification. Our project breaks new ground by integrating the concept of consolidation into segregation research.

We do this through three interlinked work packages (WPs), all utilizing comprehensive Swedish register data. WP1 maps consolidation in schools, workplaces, and neighborhoods, showing how socioeconomic, ethnic and gender compositions are correlated in these different domains. WP2 uses discrete choice models and agentbased simulations to examine how preference-based and network-based sorting mechanisms respond to varying levels of consolidation, shaping segregation patterns. WP3 investigates how the multidimensional nature of segregation, through its contextual effects, influences social inequality in terms of educational attainment and labor market outcomes.
title: Consolidated segregation: How coalescing social differences shape the causes and consequences of segregation

abstract:
People and the contexts they occupy vary in many dimensions---ethnicity, gender, income, education---that shape life experiences, social ties, aspirations, and opportunities. Segregation research examines how the composition of contexts varies across times, places, and individuals. But it often examines contextual variations in a single dimension at a time, e.g., focusing on ethnic segregation separately from income segregation. This overlooks how these aspects of segregation overlap and intersect. Macro-structural social theory and social network research suggest the overlaps and intersections between different social characteristics---referred to as consolidation---are critical to processes of stratification. Our project breaks new ground by integrating the concept of consolidation into segregation research.

We do this through three interlinked work packages (WPs), all utilizing comprehensive Swedish register data. WP1 maps consolidation in schools, workplaces, and neighborhoods, showing how socioeconomic, ethnic and gender compositions are correlated in these different domains. WP3 investigates how the multidimensional nature of segregation, through its contextual effects, influences social inequality in terms of educational attainment and labor market outcomes.

note:
Grant type: Research Project Grant

title: Consolidated segregation: How reinforcing social differences shape the causes and consequences of segregation

abstract:
We propose studying how parental influence is channeled through labor market institutions to determine offspring's occupations. Patterns of intergenerational mobility--the strength and extent of parental influence on their offspring's occupations--are a serious concern. A world in which children mostly inherit their parents' occupations contradicts the principle of equal opportunity. High inheritance may discourage workers from investing in education, hamper firms seeking competent workers, and undermine economic development.

Despite intergenerational mobility's significance, few studies have examined how labor market institutions, like industries and firms, intervene in the process. We remedy this oversight and explore parental influence on offspring's (1) initial occupational choice and skill development, (2) attainment within organizations' occupational hierarchies, and (3) inter-metropolitan migration and occupational mobility.

We link together population-based register data that describe the labor market histories of a generation of parents and their children. The data detail mobility across occupations, organizations and geographic regions. We analyze these data using advanced statistical techniques (e.g., multilevel panel models), and provide an unprecedented view of labor market channels of intergenerational mobility. Given the unique, detailed data available, Sweden should be at the forefront of studying the mechanisms of intergenerational occupational mobility.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Intergenerational Occupational Mobility in Sweden: Determinants, Mechanisms and Outcomes

abstract:
Previous studies suggest that individuals’ subjective perception of the reputation of schools and residential areas influences their decisions about where to live and which school to attend. We however know little about the nature of those reputations, how they come about, and how they eventually might impact the segregation of schools and neighborhoods. This project extends previous work by providing a holistic perspective of how the school-neighborhood portrayals in newspapers and online platforms affect the patterns of school and residential segregation.

The first stage of the project will focus on evaluating the reputation itself. Using data from digital social-media sites and the Swedish newspapers, we will measure school and neighborhood reputations in Stockholm city. Then, we will analyze which characteristics of the schools and neighborhoods contribute to shaping their reputations and whether those are results of media bias. The second stage will evaluate the effect of reputation on segregation patterns. Using Swedish register data on residential mobility and education, we will assess how reputation influences the neighborhood and school choices across different ethnic and socioeconomic groups. Lastly, we will implement a simulation platform to assess the cumulative effect of reputation on segregation and gauge the potential impact of integrative policy initiatives based on changing schools and neighborhood portrayal in the media.
title: The Role of Media in School and Neighborhood Reputation Formation, and its Effect on School and Residential Segregation
How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of second-generation migrants and natives in Stockholm.
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search

collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of second-generation migrants and natives in Stockholm
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search

note:
Grant type: Research Infrastructure Grant
Decision: Needs audit closed
title: An Integrated Register Database for Analysis of City Planning and Land Use
title-short: An Integrated Register Database for Analysis of City Planning and Land Use

abstract:
This project examines whether and how ethnic differences in neighborhood awareness and preconceptions perpetuate segregation by shaping the housing search behaviors of immigrants and natives in Uppsala. It uses a survey including a Discrete Choice Experiment, apartment search data, register data, and qualitative interviews.

Segregation is often viewed as stemming from socioeconomic inequality, housing discrimination, and ethnic preferences. Yet, despite the slow moderation of prejudices, improvements in minority socioeconomic standing, and the enactment of anti-discrimination laws, residential segregation between different ethnic groups remains high.

The emerging social structural sorting perspective attempts to explain the surprising durability of segregation, while complementing existing understandings of segregation processes. It builds on evidence that people not only lack complete knowledge of their residential options, but also that the knowledge they have is shaped by their ethnically segregated lived experiences and social networks. This leads people to exclude, in ethnically stratified ways, whole sections of cities from consideration before housing search begins, contributing to a self-perpetuating cycle of segregation.

This project engages with the forefront of segregation research through one of the most comprehensive empirical examinations of social structural sorting to date. It will contribute with useful insights to tackle segregation and inequality in the city.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of second generation migrants and natives in Stockholm
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search

abstract: This project examines when and under what circumstances same-sex families---i.e., families with young children whose parents are same-sex---move or migrate, and what kinds of neighborhoods they move to. A key question is whether same-sex families take the proximity of other same-sex families into account when deciding if or where to move, and how this thinking is related to perceptions of favorable social environments for both parents and children in these families. The study undertakes this research using a combination of Swedish register data and a novel survey instrument. The goal is to contribute to knowledge of migration practices in an understudied group, and to guide the development of policies to support parents and children in same-sex families.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Social exposure as a motive for migration? The migration strategies and migration patterns of same-sex parent families
title-short: Social exposure as a motive for migration

abstract:
Family members are important actors in the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed social welfare institutions such as Sweden. Families may be particularly important sources of instrumental, financial, and emotional support for the foreign born and their offspring, who may struggle to navigate social welfare systems, while often coping with challenging labor and housing markets. While there is evidence that immigrant families living in Europe show higher levels of family support provision and solidarity, less is known about the social structures that shape this support. In particular, relatively little is known about how proximity to family differs between immigrant and non-immigrant groups. Proximity is a key determinant of the opportunity structure of family ties, guiding whether, how often, and what type of support is exchanged among family members.

This project examines differences in the patterns and correlates of family proximity between individuals with immigrant and non-immigrant backgrounds in Sweden, focusing on the 1973-77 birth cohorts and their parents. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research with life course theory and a “linked lives” perspective drawn from family demography, we consider how proximity to family affects and is affected by residential mobility, and how this reciprocal relationship differs between immigrant and nonimmigrant families. In the process, we examine how key labor market and demographic events modify the salience of kin proximity to mobility, and how housing market resource deficits among immigrant families influence patterns of kin proximity. We do so by applying state-of-the art approaches to relational data analysis and statistical models of residential mobility to Swedish register data. Our results have implications for policies aimed at facilitating transitions to adulthood, enhancing the wellbeing of older parents, and improving the integration of immigrants and their children.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 6,000,000 SEK
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: Proximity to family shapes the opportunity structure for family solidarity and the provision of intergenerational support. Immigrant families coping with housing market resource deficits and labor market challenges are particularly likely to need or call upon family support and may be more likely to live near kin. Yet little is known about differences in family proximity between individuals with and without immigrant backgrounds in Sweden. This project examines group differences in the patterns and correlates of family proximity, focusing on the 197377 birth cohorts and their parents. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research with life course theory and a “linked lives” perspective drawn from family demography, we consider how proximity to family affects and is affected by residential mobility, and how this reciprocal relationship differs between immigrant and non-immigrant families. In the process, we examine how key labor market and demographic events modify the salience of kin proximity for mobility decisions, and how housing market resource deficits among immigrants influence distance to kin. We do so by applying state-of-the art approaches to relational data analyses and statistical models of residential mobility to data from Swedish population registers. Our results have implications for policies aimed at facilitating transitions to adulthood, improving the integration of immigrants and their offspring, and reducing residential segregation.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: Family members are vital to the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed welfare institutions. Families are particularly important sources of instrumental, financial, and emotional support for immigrants and their offspring, who have more limited social networks and greater challenges navigating labor and housing markets. Although there is evidence that immigrant families living in Europe show higher levels of family support and solidarity, less is known about proximity between family members, which shapes whether, how often, and what type of support is exchanged among family members. This project examines differences in and the determinants of family proximity for immigrant and non-immigrant families. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research with a “linked lives” perspective drawn from family demography and life course research, we consider how proximity to family affects residential mobility and how key labor market and demographic events for both older parents and their adult children modify the salience of kin proximity for mobility.
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: Family members are vital to the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed welfare institutions. Families are particularly important sources of support for immigrants and their offspring, who have more limited social networks and greater challenges navigating labor and housing markets. Although there is evidence that immigrant families show higher levels of family support, less is known about proximity between kin, which guides the “structure” of family support. This project examines differences in and the determinants of family proximity for immigrant and nonimmigrant families. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research with a “linked lives” perspective from family demography and life course research, we study the relationship between parent-child proximity and residential mobility by applying discrete choice models using Swedish register data. We focus on the 1940-52 birth cohorts for older parents and the 1973-83 birth cohorts for offspring. We compare the effects of proximity to family between immigrant and nonimmigrant groups, consider life course events as moderators that may enhance the importance of kin proximity for residential choices, and examine differences in these moderation effects between groups. Our results have important implications for policies aimed at enhancing the wellbeing of families and improving the integration of immigrants and their children.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract:
Family members are vital to the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed welfare institutions. Families may be particularly important sources of social support for immigrants and their offspring, whose reliance on kin may aid in the settlement process. Although immigrant families living in Europe show higher levels of family support and solidarity, less is known about proximity between family members. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research and life course research, we consider how proximity to family affects residential mobility and how key labor market and demographic events for older parents and their adult children modify the salience of kin proximity for mobility.

This mixed-methods study examines the relationship between proximity and mobility by triangulating across three different approaches. First, we use Swedish register data to describe patterns of intergenerational proximity and kin availability in immigrant and non-immigrant families. We next estimate models to assess how proximity to family relates to residential choices. Second, we use survey data from the Swedish Gender and Generations Survey (GGS) to understand how the quality of intergenerational ties relates to mobility intentions across families of immigrant and non-immigrant background. Finally, we use multi-generation, qualitative interviews of immigrant and non-immigrant families to gain a more nuanced understanding of the life-course dynamics of family relationships, mobility, and residential proximity.
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: Family members are vital to the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed welfare institutions. Families may be particularly important sources of social support for immigrants and their offspring, who may rely on kin during the settlement process. However, little is known about proximity between family members by immigrant background. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research and life course research, we describe how proximity to family varies across immigrant and non-immigrant groups and how it affects residential mobility. We examine how key life course events for older parents and their adult children modify the salience of kin proximity for mobility, doing so separately for different groups. We use a mixed-methods approach, triangulating across statistical analyses of register and survey data, and qualitative interviews. We use Swedish register data to describe patterns of intergenerational proximity and kin availability in immigrant and non-immigrant families and use discrete choice models to assess how proximity to family relates to residential choices. We use survey data from the Swedish Gender and Generations Survey (GGS) to understand how the quality of intergenerational ties relates to mobility intentions. Finally, multigeneration, qualitative interviews with immigrant and non-immigrant families give us more nuanced insight into the life-course dynamics of family relationships that give rise to residential proximity.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: Family members are vital to the social safety net, even in countries with well-developed welfare institutions. Families may be particularly important sources of social support for immigrants and their offspring, who may rely on kin during the settlement process. However, little is known about proximity between family members by immigrant background. Joining together analytical techniques from segregation research and life course research, we describe how proximity to family varies across immigrant and non-immigrant groups and how it affects residential mobility. We examine how key life course events for older parents and their adult children modify the salience of kin proximity for mobility, doing so separately for different groups. We use a mixed methods approach, triangulating across statistical analyses of register and survey data, and qualitative interviews. We use Swedish register data to describe patterns of intergenerational proximity and kin availability in immigrant and non-immigrant families and use discrete choice models to assess how proximity to family relates to residential choices. We use survey data from the Swedish Gender and Generations Survey (GGS) to understand how the quality of intergenerational ties relates to mobility intentions and choices. Finally, multigeneration, qualitative interviews with immigrant and non-immigrant families give us more nuanced insight into the life-course dynamics of family relationships that give rise to residential proximity.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Linked lives, residential mobility, and differences in spatial proximity to kin by immigrant background

abstract: The purpose of this research environment is to explain how the dynamics of residential and school segregation shape ethnic and socioeconomic inequalities in academic outcomes, and suggest policy remedies. Despite extensive research on the causes and consequences of segregation, we still have surprisingly little knowledge about how the two fit together. This is partly due to complex relationships between them. When parents choose a place to live and send their children to school, it is both influenced by and influences the composition of schools and neighborhoods. These compositions can affect short-term achievements among students, which guide subsequent educational choices and shape academic outcomes. This interplay means that structural interventions in school and housing policies–changes in the quantity, quality, and spatial distribution of schools and housing, as well as the rules governing access to them–can lead to sudden compositional shifts and unintended consequences. To address these complexities, we form an interdisciplinary team with unique methodological, substantive, and theoretical expertise. We use Swedish register data to estimate state-of-the-art models of residential and school choices and causal models of school and neighborhood effects. We integrate our findings into generative simulations and novel visualizations of segregation processes, using these to vet potential policy interventions that we theorize will reduce segregation and inequality.
title: Neighborhood and school segregation across educational careers: patterns, processes, and implications for educational inequality

abstract: Recent welfare state retrenchment has shifted Swedish housing policy towards market-based approaches, increasing the importance of socioeconomic resources for residential attainment. Yet, the extent of public intervention in the housing market remains considerable, as senioritybased, non-market housing queues continue to play an important role. Both market and nonmarket housing allocation mechanisms may contribute to segregation by disadvantaging newly arrived immigrants who necessarily lack seniority and who often have few economic resources. This project investigates how and to what extent ethnic groups are unevenly distributed across market and non-market parts of the housing sector and how this relates to residential segregation. We analyze temporal and intermunicipal variation in ethnic tenure segmentation patterns in large and medium-sized Swedish municipalities, and how differences in segmentation and recent changes in tenure mix relate to overall ethnic segregation. We then delve into the individual-level dynamics of residential mobility. In particular, we identify the selection processes contributing to intergroup differences in the distribution and transition of individuals across different housing tenures and neighborhoods. Finally, we use microsimulation methods to examine how policy interventions in the housing market influence household mobility and induce changes in macro-level patterns of ethnic segregation across and within market and non-market sectors.
collection-title: Targeted Call - Housing Policy for Social Sustainability
title: Segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms
title-short: Segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms

abstract: Recent welfare state reforms have shifted Swedish housing policy towards market-based approaches, increasing the importance of economic resources for residential attainment. Yet, the extent of public intervention in the housing market remains important, as seniority-based non-market housing queues continue to play an important role in housing allocation. Both market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms contribute to segregation by disadvantaging newly arrived immigrants who have few economic resources and lack seniority. This project investigates how and to what extent ethnic groups are unevenly distributed across the different housing tenures in Swedish major cities and large municipalities, and how this relates to residential segregation. We study how intermunicipal differences in housing tenure composition and mix relate to overall ethnic residential segregation patterns. We then delve into the individual-level dynamics of residential mobility, and we identify the selection processes contributing to differences between immigrants and native Swedes in the distribution and transition of individuals across different housing tenures and neighborhoods. Finally, we use microsimulation to examine how actual and counterfactual policy interventions in local housing markets influence residential mobility choices and thereby induce changes in patterns and levels of ethnic residential segregation in municipalities, across and within the market and non-market regulated sectors.
collection-title: Targeted Call - Research Project in Sustainable Spatial Planning
title: The segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms
title-short: Segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms

abstract: Recent welfare state reforms have shifted Swedish housing policy towards market-based approaches, increasing the importance of socioeconomic resources for residential attainment. Yet, the extent of public intervention in the housing market remains considerable, as seniority-based, non-market housing queues continue to play an important role in housing allocation. Both market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms may contribute to segregation by disadvantaging newly arrived immigrants who necessarily lack seniority and who often have few economic resources. This project investigates how and to what extent ethnic groups are unevenly distributed across market and non-market segments of the housing market and how this relates to residential segregation. We analyze variation in the distribution of immigrant groups and native Swedes over the different types of housing (owner occupied, tenant owned, public rental, and private rental) over time across major cities and large municipalities in Sweden. We also study how intermunicipal differences in tenure composition of the housing stock and changes in housing tenure mix relate to overall ethnic residential segregation patterns. We then delve into the individual-level dynamics of residential mobility. In particular, we identify the selection processes contributing to differences between immigrants and native Swedes in the distribution and transition of individuals across different housing tenures and neighborhoods. Finally, we use computer simulation (microsimulation) to examine how actual and counterfactual policy interventions in the local housing markets influence households’ residential mobility choices and thereby induce changes in patterns and levels of ethnic residential segregation in the municipalities, across and within the market and non-market regulated sectors.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 5,796,044 SEK
title: Segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms.
title-short: Segregating effects of market and non-market housing allocation mechanisms

abstract: This project examines the link between social networks, including kinship and school friendships, and the residential segregation of immigrant groups in Sweden, with a focus on second generation immigrants. Kin and friends influence a number of social and demographic behaviors, including migration. At the same time, social networks are partly a result of existing residential segregation. These two considerations suggest a reciprocal process of segregation whereby existing uneven spatial distributions of immigrant groups beget ethnically and spatially divided social networks, which enable the reproduction of segregation through their influence on migration. We combine register data that identify family ties, school attendance, and residential mobility with survey data characterizing friendship networks within schools. We use advanced network analysis and discrete choice statistical methods to investigate how school attendance is influenced by residential segregation, how schools shape friendship networks, and how these friendship networks, along with existing kinship ties, influence residential mobility. We then use micro-simulations to show how these components fit together to generate residential segregation observed at the city-level. This project is one of the first to explicitly connect the literature on social networks and the literature on residential segregation. It makes extensive use of Swedish population registers and provides an unprecedented view of social processes contributing to the intergenerational transmission of context and the production of residential segregation. The project provides important insights for policy makers, showing how interventions designed to address segregation among first generation immigrants may have long-term consequences for their children, may forestall the development of an ethnicized underclass, and increase wellbeing for immigrants and native Swedes.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 5,398,746 SEK
title: Social Ties and Residential Segregation

abstract: This project examines the link between social networks, including kinship and school friendships, and the residential segregation of immigrant groups in Sweden, with a focus on second generation immigrants. We combine register data that identify family ties, school attendance, and residential mobility with survey data characterizing friendship networks within schools. We use advanced network analysis and discrete choice statistical methods to investigate how school attendance is influenced by residential segregation, how schools shape friendship networks, and how these friendship networks, along with existing kinship ties, influence residential mobility. We then use micro-simulations to show how these components fit together to generate residential segregation observed at the city-level. This project is one of the first to explicitly connect the literature on social networks and the literature on residential segregation. It makes extensive use of Swedish population registers and provides an unprecedented view of social processes contributing to the intergenerational transmission of context and the production of residential segregation. The project provides important insights for policy makers, showing how interventions designed to address segregation among first generation immigrants may have long-term consequences for their children, may forestall the development of an ethnicized underclass, and increase wellbeing for immigrants and native Swedes.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
note:
Grant type: Research Project Grant
Decision: Granted
Budget: 5,441,463 SEK
title: Social Ties and Residential Segregation

abstract:
This project examines how proximity to kin is reciprocally related to intergenerational relationships and the spatial integration of immigrants in Sweden. Among second-generation immigrants, close residential proximity to parents may provide vital access to social support, but it may also concentrate social interactions within ethnically delimited networks that constrain residential, labor market, and other opportunities. Conversely, moving away from parents may facilitate integration, but can strain intergenerational relationships and disrupt support between generations.

By combining register, survey, and interview data, this project develops a dynamic, multigenerational account of the evolving tension between family relationships, residential proximity, and immigrant integration. Register data allow us to map kin availability and proximity across the life course for second-generation immigrants and non-immigrants, which we incorporate into discrete choice models of residential destinations to identify when and how life-course transitions—such as nest-leaving and union formation—produce differences in proximity to kin. Panel data from the Swedish Gender and Generations Survey tell us how family obligations, relationship quality, and support exchange shape mobility intentions and subsequent moves. Finally, qualitative interviews with parents and adult children in immigrant and non-immigrant families reveal the mechanisms linking family ties, mobility, and integration.
note:
Grant type: Research Project Grant
Budget: 5,030,600 SEK
title: The Spatial Bargain: Intergenerational Proximity and the Integration of Second-Generation Immigrants

abstract: This project investigates the reciprocal relationship between residential segregation and the housing landscape—the geographic mix of different tenure types and densities. In so doing, the project shifts the focus of segregation research from the demand-side—the renters and homeowners vying for places to live—to the supply side: the landlords and developers who transform the housing landscape by building, renovating, converting, and selling housing. Using detailed registry data describing the Swedish population, properties, and building permits, this project uses statistical analyses and computer simulations to investigate a series of research questions designed to explore the relationship between housing and segregation. How is mobility related to the spatial distribution of different housing tenures? Are decisions about where to build housing, what housing is built, or whether public housing is sold to private actors, related to the social characteristics of neighborhoods? And how have recent changes in the Swedish housing landscape, particularly the swing toward privatization, contributed to or mitigated patterns of mobility that lead to segregation? Our results and analytical methods will be of keen interest to national and municipal policy makers looking to reduce segregation while ensuring that all Swedish residents have access to high quality housing.
title: Transforming the housing landscape: how dwelling construction and tenure conversion affect processes of residential segregation

note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 5,202,517 SEK
title: Like candidate, like voter: the role of life trajectories and representativeness of right wing radical populist candidates in Sweden

collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: Like candidate, like voter: the role of life trajectories and representativeness of right wing radical populist candidates in Sweden

abstract: This research environment addresses how media discourses have portrayed and reacted to immigration, during a period of heightened immigration to Sweden. It is particularly concerned with attention in traditional and social media to the arrival of refugee and assylum seekers during the 2000s and 2010s, often from countries in Africa and the Middle East. The environment brings together interdisciplinary researchers to develop and deploy cutting-edge, machine-learning based text analysis models. These methods are applied to large-scale text data drawn from popular Swedish social media websites, political speeches, and stores of articles published in Swedish newspapers and maintained by Sweden's national library, Kungliga biblioteket. We use the models to extract the evolving semantic meanings attached to immigration in the public discourse, relating these to prominent events and the socio-demographic attributes of immigrant groups.
title: Mining for Meaning: The Dynamics of Public Discourse on Migration

abstract: LINGUA-MAP investigates multilingual communication practices on Swedish public institutions’ websites and their implications for integration. Combining computational, qualitative, and quantitative approaches, the project maps the presence of languages other than Swedish in such sources and assesses if such provision supports social inclusion. First, we provide an overview of the multilingual communication landscape through interactive visualizations and computational analyses of public institutions’ websites. We identify patterns of language coverage, balance, and content parallelism, as well as consistency within and across institutions. Informed by these patterns, we then conduct in‑depth qualitative case studies of selected institutions' websites, focusing on the English‑language content. We examine translation strategies, content parallelism, structural choices, and overall linguistic quality, assessing how information is adapted-or not-for different target audiences. Third, we link patterns of multilingual provision to integration outcomes using Swedish population registers. Using statistical and machine‑learning models, we examine if access to multilingual content is associated with outcomes such as labor market participation and education, exploiting variation across time, place and language groups. LINGUA-MAP provides important evidence on the role of multilingual institutional communication in promoting equitable access to public services and immigrant integration.
note:
Grant type: Research Project Grant
Budget: 5,091,000 SEK
title: Language as a Gatekeeper? Mapping and Analysis of Multilingual Public Information and Social Integration in Sweden (LINGUA-MAP)

abstract: This project assesses the extent to which the residential segregation of immigrants in Swedish cities defines broad ethnic categories that, in turn, guide patterns of social tie formation and migration that determine the shape of ethnic segregation to come.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 5,869,187 SEK
title: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation: patterns, dynamics and behaviours
title-short: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation

abstract: The project assesses the extent to which residential proximity among immigrant groups in Sweden favours the crossing of ethnic boundaries and the consolidation of pan-ethnic identities. We use register-based data for identifying nascent pan-ethnic groups by analysing the patterns of spatial integration and segregation of countryof-origin groups. Based on the so-identified pan-ethnicities, we first examine trends in metro-level segregation and neighbourhood change from a multigroup perspective. We then perform a network analysis on survey data on friendship ties in Swedish schools to seek evidence of pan-ethnic homophily. Next, we use conditional logistic regression to estimate the effect of neighbourhoods’ pan-ethnic composition on individuals’ residential mobility decisions. Sweden lacks a recognized standard for the pan-ethnic aggregation of country-of-origin data. This leads to inconsistent definitions of pan-ethnicities that complicate cross-study comparisons. With this project, we propose developing a standard based on extant segregation patterns in neighbourhoods and friendship networks. Pan-ethnic categorization has also important consequences for implementation of policies for tackling residential segregation. A lack of attention to the extensible nature of ethnic boundaries may lead policymakers to overlook critical changes in identity formation processes that influence immigrants’ social networks and affect their prospects for socio-spatial integration in cities.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation: patterns, dynamics and behaviours
title-short: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation

abstract: Pan-ethnic categorization is a necessary, albeit often implicit, criterion in the design of policies against unequal opportunities and affirmative actions to prevent discrimination. Such measures presuppose the identification of target groups that are usually represented by specific segments of the immigrant population. Yet an inadequate demarcation of target groups may undermine the effectiveness of such initiatives. This issue concerns residential segregation research in particular. Studies on residential segregation usually collapse immigrant groups defined by country of birth together into broader pan-ethnic categories. Yet, the absence of a recognized standard for the aggregation of country-of-birth groups complicates the comparison of findings across residential segregation studies. At the same time, existing research is characterized by a lack of attention to the flexible nature of nationality-defined identity boundaries in immigrants’ identity formation processes, which in turn affect their social networks and prospects for social integration. We propose developing a standard for a pan-ethnic categorization of immigrant groups in Sweden based on extant residential segregation patterns. The project assesses the extent to which residential proximity among immigrant groups favours the crossing of nationality defined identity boundaries and the consolidation of broader pan-ethnic identities. We use register-data for identifying nascent pan-ethnic categories by analysing the patterns of spatial integration and segregation of detailed county-of-birth groups, and their changes over time from a multi-group perspective. We then use conditional logistic regression to estimate the effect of neighbourhood pan-ethnic composition on individuals’ residential mobility decisions. Finally, use log-linear and log-multiplicative models to analyse intermarriage patterns between first-generation immigrants and seek evidence for pan-ethnic homogamy.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 4,995,845 SEK
title: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation: patterns, dynamics and behaviour
title-short: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation

abstract: This project examines how pan-ethnic relations can affect patterns of segregation, using Swedish cities as a test bed for theory. Pan-ethnicity relates to a sense of common cause, or even just a greater tolerance for contact, with ethnic groups that are culturally adjacent to one's own. Pan-ethnic relations seem particularly relevant for immigrants in contexts where their own ethnic group is relatively rare, but culturally similar groups may be more abundant. Theoretically, the project shows that when groups have pan-ethnic affinities, in addition to homophilous preferences for members of their own ethnic group, unique segregation dynamics and patterns can emerge. Empirically, we demonstrate the existence of these pan-ethnic affinities in Sweden using data on residential mobility, and we work out how the specific configuration of ethnic and pan-ethnic sorting behaviors we observe relate to patterns of segregation.
title: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation: patterns and dynamics
title-short: The pan-ethnic dimension of residential segregation

abstract: Housing policies designed to reduce economic segregation in Sweden have been largely effective but may have inadvertently contributed to ethnic segregation. The rental queue system and collectively bargained rental prices are important mechanisms for guaranteeing the right to a good home for all residents, but also limit housing choices for newcomers who are at the bottom of queues that are often years-long. Compounding this problem, refugee arrivals are disadvantaged in the private housing market because they often lack the financial resources to purchase a privately-owned housing unit. This project investigates how and to what extent ethnic groups are unevenly distributed across rentals and ownership in major Swedish cities and how this affects patterns of residential segregation. We identify the selection processes that lead to differences between native-born Swedes and immigrants in the types of housing units they occupy as well as the neighborhoods they are located in. Finally, we test the impact of housing reform by using computer simulations to examine how actual and counterfactual policy interventions in local housing markets might influence residential mobility and thereby induce changes in patterns and levels of ethnic residential segregation.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: The Effects of Public and Private Housing Markets on Ethnic Segregation

abstract:
This project examines whether and how differences in neighborhood awareness and preconceptions perpetuate residential segregation by shaping the housing search of immigrants and natives in Stockholm. It uses surveybased experiments, analysis of register data, and qualitative interviews.

Residential segregation is often viewed as stemming from socioeconomic inequality, housing discrimination, and ethnically differentiated residential preferences. Yet, despite the slow moderation of prejudices, improvements in minority socioeconomic standing, and the enactment of anti-discrimination laws, segregation between groups defined by ethnicity and immigrant background remains high.

The emerging social structural sorting theory attempts to explain segregation’s unexpected durability while complementing existing understandings of segregation processes. It builds on evidence that people not only lack complete knowledge of their residential options, but also that what knowledge they have is shaped by their segregated lived experiences and networks. This leads people to exclude, in ethnically stratified ways, whole sections of cities from consideration before housing search begins, contributing to self-perpetuating cycles of segregation.

This project engages with the forefront of segregation research through one of the most comprehensive empirical examinations of social structural sorting to date. We show how this new perspective can be combined with existing perspectives to enhance our understanding of segregation, while providing new insights into interventions that may mitigate segregation and reduce inequalities.
note:
Decision: Not accepted for stage two
Budget: 5,998,312
title: How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of migrants and natives in Stockholm.
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search

abstract:
This project examines whether and how differences in neighborhood awareness and preconceptions perpetuate residential segregation by shaping the housing search of immigrants and natives in Stockholm. It uses surveybased experiments, analysis of register data, and qualitative interviews.

Residential segregation is often viewed as stemming from socioeconomic inequality, housing discrimination, and ethnically differentiated residential preferences. Yet, despite the slow moderation of prejudices, improvements in minority socioeconomic standing, and the enactment of anti-discrimination laws, segregation between ethnic groups remains high.

The emerging social structural sorting theory attempts to explain segregation’s unexpected durability while complementing existing understandings of segregation processes. It builds on evidence that people not only lack complete knowledge of their residential options, but also that what knowledge they have is shaped by their segregated lived experiences and social networks. This leads people to exclude, in ethnically stratified ways, whole sections of cities from consideration before housing search begins, contributing to self-perpetuating cycles of segregation.

This project engages with the forefront of segregation research through one of the most comprehensive empirical examinations of social structural sorting to date. This new perspective will be combined with existing perspectives to enhance our understanding of segregation.
collection-title: Research Project - Humanities and Social Sciences
title: How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of migrants and natives in Stockholm.
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search

abstract: The social structural sorting perspective posits that group-specific 'blind-spots' contribute to the persistence of ethnic residential segregation. If people learn about the city through segregated lived experiences and networks, their knowledge and perceptions about residential options will differ along ethnic lines. They will exclude, often in ethnically stratified ways, whole sections of cities from their housing search even when it is not their intention.  We test this framework in Sweden. We exame whether natives and immigrants are aware of the same neighborhoods in the cities where they live. We also ask if perceptions of the neighborhoods people are aware of systematically differ between groups, and how these perceptions and the blind spots themselves relate to lived experiences and the social relationships people have with friends, family, and acquantainces. We do so through in-depth interviews, the development and collection of new survey data about neighborhood perceptions and housing search, and the analyses of the survey data in conjunction with population data drawn from the Swedish population registers.
title: How do perceptions of the city inform neighbourhood selection and housing search? A comparison of migrants and natives in Stockholm
title-short: Perceptions of the city and housing search
